planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in early spring in moist, acidic, well-drained soil with a pH of 4.5–6.0 in partial shade to full sun; sourwood is naturally an understory tree in the Appalachians and is remarkably shade tolerant when young, making it an excellent choice beneath high-canopy oaks and hickories. It resents root disturbance and transplants best while young from containers; avoid alkaline soils and compacted clay. Top-dress annually with pine bark or leaf mulch to maintain soil acidity and moisture.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil; sourwood is among the most multi-season native trees of eastern North America, earning its place in the landscape with four distinct seasons of interest — it is a member of the heath family (Ericaceae) and one of the few trees in that family to achieve canopy stature. In July, when most trees offer nothing but foliage, sourwood erupts in long, arching, drooping panicles of small white urn-shaped flowers reminiscent of lily-of-the-valley — these are the primary source of the prized sourwood honey produced by Appalachian beekeepers, considered by many to be the finest American varietal honey. Fall color is nothing short of spectacular: leaves turn vivid scarlet to deep crimson while the persistent creamy seed capsules hang in graceful clusters, often coinciding with yellow foliage of neighboring trees for a striking contrast. The glossy, lance-shaped leaves have a pleasantly sour taste (hence the common name) and the tree's naturally layered branching habit provides excellent winter structure; it is exceptionally long-lived, low-maintenance, and virtually pest-free when sited in appropriately acidic soils.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — NC State University Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped stock in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; two-winged silverbell is native to rich, moist woodland slopes and streambanks of the southeastern Coastal Plain and performs best where afternoon shade moderates summer heat. It is more heat-tolerant than Halesia carolina and is the preferred silverbell for zones 8–9 gardens. Seed requires a double dormancy — warm stratification for 3 months followed by cold stratification for 3 months — and may take two seasons to germinate; container transplants are strongly recommended.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil; two-winged silverbell is a graceful southeastern native small tree that puts on one of the most charming spring flower displays of any native woody plant — in March and April, before or just as the leaves emerge, the pendulous branches are draped in clusters of pure-white, four-petaled, nodding bell-shaped flowers that dangle like delicate ornaments along every stem, providing exceptional early nectar for pollinators. Halesia diptera is distinguished from the more commonly grown Carolina silverbell (H. carolina) by its two-winged fruits rather than four-winged, and by its superior heat tolerance, making it the silverbell of choice for gardens in the Deep South and Gulf Coast regions where H. carolina struggles. The fruits that follow are decorative in their own right, persisting on the branches well into fall, and the clean oval foliage turns yellow in autumn before dropping. An outstanding specimen tree for woodland garden edges, naturalistic streamside plantings, and residential lawns in zones 5–9 where a refined, low-maintenance flowering tree of genuine native provenance is desired.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — NC State University Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in moist to consistently moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; American snowbell is native to streambanks, wet woodland margins, and boggy lowlands of the eastern and southeastern United States and performs best where roots can access reliable moisture throughout the growing season. It tolerates periodic brief flooding and is an excellent choice for rain gardens and shaded streamside plantings. Seed requires 3 months of cold moist stratification; container transplants establish more reliably.

directions: Plant in partial shade in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil; American snowbell is a refined and underappreciated native shrub of eastern North America, bearing in May and June an elegant display of nodding, white, reflexed-petaled flowers with conspicuous bright-yellow stamens — the blossoms hang in short racemes beneath the arching stems, reminiscent of a smaller, shrubby version of the ornamental Japanese snowbell (Styrax japonicus) but entirely native and better adapted to wet sites. Styrax americanus is a multi-stemmed, loosely branched shrub native to moist, low-lying woodland margins, floodplain forests, and streambanks from Virginia to Florida and west to Missouri, making it an ideal choice for rain gardens, bioswales, and the moist edges of naturalistic woodland gardens where many other shrubs fail. The clean, elliptic leaves emerge fresh green in spring and offer a pleasant backdrop to the delicate flowers; fall color is a quiet soft yellow. A superb native alternative to non-native snowbell species for gardeners seeking an authentically regional plant with quiet seasonal beauty, exceptional ecological value for native bees, and reliable performance in wet or seasonally flooded sites.

planting.note: Divide and plant rhizomes in spring once water temperatures warm above 60°F, placing crowns in heavy loam or clay aquatic soil in containers submerged 6–12 inches below the water surface; golden club thrives in the still or slow-moving water of ponds, streams, and freshwater wetlands and is among the most shade-tolerant of native aquatic plants. Site in partial shade to full sun — it tolerates considerably more shade than most aquatics. Established clumps can be divided every 3–4 years in spring.

directions: Plant in still to slow-moving water 4–12 inches deep in heavy, nutrient-rich aquatic soil in partial shade to full sun; golden club is one of the most distinctive and visually striking native aquatic plants of eastern North America, producing in early spring — often while other plants are barely stirring — unusual upright flower spathes consisting of a slender, bright golden-yellow spadix on a pure-white stem, held above the water surface like a glowing torch that gives the plant its evocative common name. The large, oblong, blue-green leaves have a remarkably hydrophobic waxy surface coating that causes water to bead and roll off in glistening droplets, a phenomenon known as the lotus effect, making the foliage appear perpetually dry and silvery in wet conditions. Orontium aquaticum is tolerant of considerable shade compared to most aquatics — a valuable trait for water features beneath overhanging tree canopies where many emergent plants fail to perform — and spreads gradually by rhizome to form a handsome colony. A superb choice for naturalistic ponds, rain gardens with permanent water features, and native plant water gardens throughout zones 5–10.

planting.note: Divide established clumps and plant rhizomes in spring in heavy, fertile aquatic soil in containers placed at the margin of a pond or slow stream in water 0–12 inches deep; arrow arum is one of the most adaptable native emergent wetland plants, tolerating anything from consistently wet soil at the waterline to full submersion of the crown in shallow water. It spreads readily by offsets and can form large, impenetrable colonies in shallow water — divide every 3–4 years to manage spread and maintain vigor.

directions: Plant at pond margins or in shallow water 0–12 inches deep in heavy, fertile aquatic or wetland soil in partial shade to full sun; arrow arum is a bold and architecturally striking native wetland perennial of eastern North America, producing large, glossy, prominently veined, arrowhead-shaped leaves up to 24 inches long that rise from the water on stout stems and create a dramatic vertical accent in naturalistic water gardens, constructed wetlands, and pond edges throughout zones 5–9. The flowers are inconspicuous greenish spathes typical of the arum family (Araceae), emerging in late spring amid the foliage; they are followed by clusters of fleshy green berries that ripen in late summer and are an important food source for wood ducks, mallards, and other waterfowl — making arrow arum an outstanding plant for wildlife ponds and waterfowl habitat restoration projects. Peltandra virginica is not intended for ornamental flower display but earns its place through bold foliage texture, ecological value, and reliable performance in the difficult wet-edge niche where few ornamentals thrive; it is a foundational species for naturalistic freshwater wetland gardens and native pond plantings.
sources: Ducks Unlimited Native Plants for Wildlife Habitat
